originalTitle: Kevin Warsh has homed in on three key phrases. How Fed watchers interpret them
summary: In five public appearances, Warsh has used the phrase "family fight" 13 times, returning to "first principles" 11 times and "inflation is a choice" for the Fed six times.
content:
He's not quite at the level of obscurity of former Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan, but new Fed chief Kevin Warsh has settled on a few phrases that are notable for their repetition and lack of clarity.

In five public appearances, starting with his nomination hearing in April, through his first press conference, roundtable in Portugal and two congressional testimonies, Warsh has used the phrase "family fight" 13 times, returning to "first principles" 11 times and "inflation is a choice" for the Fed six times.

But what those phrases mean for monetary policy is more challenging than counting their usage. Yet, with a chairman who has decided to say less than his predecessors, there's a premium on understanding the words he does choose.

CNBC asked five close Fed watchers their views on what these three phrases mean to them.

Dan Greenhaus, strategist, Solus Alternative Asset Management:

"If Chair Warsh encourages more open debate around policy and creates an environment where prevailing assumptions can be challenged rather than simply accepted, that should ultimately lead to better policymaking. The benefit is less about changing the outcome of any individual meeting and more about improving the quality of the decision-making process over time."

Loretta Mester, former Cleveland Fed president:

"Ensuring the environment at the [Federal Open Market Committee] meeting allows for all views/arguments to be heard. I note that this was already the atmosphere when I was on the FOMC. I never felt constrained in what I said or the policy case I argued. In reality, it is 19 people around the table so there has to be some order or else maybe it is only the loudest voice in the room that gets heard and you will actually have fewer views expressed since it will be hard to jump in."

Claudia Sahm, chief economist at New Century Advisors:

"FOMC meetings tend to be highly scripted affairs, with prepared remarks read aloud and limited conversation. Warsh wants a livelier back-and-forth — a style he is more comfortable with. The format is unlikely to affect the policy decision, and 19 participants is a large group for a free-for-all conversation. "

Mark Spindel, Fed author and Potomac River Capital chief investment officer:

"To Kevin, this is his aphorism for the natural debate going on inside the FOMC and the Board. It implies obvious disagreements, but like all 'family fights' is best kept to members of the family and not revealed too much to the public. I think it's the second part that his committee colleagues (and market participants) are finding problematic, and doesn't fit the priors. Members of the committee are clearly willing to disagree in public, or at least state their opinions much more openly (than Chairman Warsh). It's also a way of deflecting his responsibility from external pressure (POTUS, CONGRESS, MARKETS)."

Michael Feroli, chief U.S. economist at JPMorgan:

"I guess the family fight is him trying to be folksy, but it doesn't depart from the tradition under [Ben] Bernanke of (publicly) welcoming dissenting opinions."

"This is vague enough to mean whatever Warsh wants it to mean, but in context, it appears to be the basis for the decision making and structural reform he is manifesting. Over the summer (in Sintra) he said his Central Banking leadership colleagues shared a 'willingness to go back to first principles' as he questioned the whole process of monetary policy making. I'd think his re-introduction of the monetary aggregates, his cute "monetary policy should have something to do with money," is cut from the same general cloth as 'first principles.' Sadly, the science of monetary policy and other workhorses of monetary policy (The Phillips Curve), economic forecasting even appear to be unhelpful in Warsh's first principled approach. He's not been shy about slamming the year-after-year misses in inflation and blaming [Jerome] Powell and company. The low level of interest rates, the balance sheet, the failure to tighten sooner, FAIT and so on, all of that (to Warsh) was a departure from 'first principles.'"

"'First principles' is code for 'question everything.' Warsh has said repeatedly that he wants "regime change" at the Fed and questioning the basic assumptions of how monetary policy is done fits that agenda. I am skeptical that Warsh will be able to rewrite first principles. Showing that an assumption is flawed is not enough; it requires offering a better replacement. Even with his task forces, Warsh is likely to come up short on new first principles — no regime change in monetary policy, but some incremental improvements to how it's done."

"Rethink the way the Fed goes about achieving its dual mandate goals of price stability and maximum employment without preconceived notions or assumptions, or precluding approaches because they may differ from the current approach. Think first about what the best approach is for communications, inflation and labor market assessment, balance sheet and operating framework, and data sources. Then consider how to transition to these new approaches."

"Note that the Committee has tended not to want to make major changes — consider the number of times the statement changed only by one or two words. This rethinking from first principles frees the committee to consider new approaches rather than minor tweaks. They will then have to think about how best to transition there."

"The 'first principles' line feels like it has the same spirit as his remark about PhDs from elite institutions not keeping money in monetary policy. Both seem to suggest the institution has let the academically driven types distract the Fed from focusing on a few basic economic principles. (Powell also didn't have a PhD but he certainly seemed less defensive about it!)"

"Getting back to first principles could have significant implications for policymaking. What is the Fed's mandate, and how should it go about fulfilling that mandate? Whether one agrees with this
chineseTitle: 凯文·沃什 重点关注了三个关键短语。美联储观察人士如何解读它们
chineseSummary: 在五次公开露面中，沃什13次使用“家庭斗争”一词，11次回归“第一原则”，6次“通胀是美联储的选择”。
chineseContent:
他并不完全达到美联储前主席艾伦·格林斯潘的默默无闻的水平，但新任美联储主席凯文·沃什已经确定了一些因其重复和缺乏清晰度而引人注目的短语。

从4月份的提名听证会开始，到他的第一次新闻发布会、葡萄牙圆桌会议和两次国会证词，沃什在五次公开露面中13次使用“家庭斗争”一词，11次回归“第一原则”，6次“通胀是美联储的选择”。

但这些短语对货币政策的意义比计算它们的使用情况更具挑战性。然而，对于一位决定比前任少说话的董事长来说，理解他所选择的措辞就显得尤为重要。

CNBC 询问了五位美联储密切观察人士对这三个短语对他们意味着什么的看法。

Solus 另类资产管理公司策略师 Dan Greenhaus：

“如果沃什主席鼓励围绕政策进行更公开的辩论，并创造一个可以挑战而不是简单地接受普遍假设的环境，那么最终应该会带来更好的政策制定。好处不是改变任何个别会议的结果，而是随着时间的推移提高决策过程的质量。”

克利夫兰联储前行长洛雷塔·梅斯特：

“确保[联邦公开市场委员会]会议的环境允许所有观点/论点被听到。我注意到，这已经是我在联邦公开市场委员会任职时的氛围。我从来没有对我所说的话或我争论的政策案例感到受限。实际上，会议桌周围有19个人，所以必须有某种秩序，否则可能只有房间里最响亮的声音被听到，而你实际上表达的观点会更少，因为很难加入。”

新世纪顾问公司首席经济学家克劳迪娅·萨姆（Claudia Sahm）：

“FOMC 会议往往是高度照本宣科的事务，大声朗读事先准备好的发言，对话也很有限。沃什想要更活跃的来回对话——这是他更喜欢的风格。这种形式不太可能影响政策决定，而且 19 名与会者对于一场自由对话来说是一个很大的群体。”

美联储作者兼 Potomac River Capital 首席投资官 Mark Spindel：

“对凯文来说，这是他对 FOMC 和董事会内部自然辩论的格言。这意味着明显的分歧，但就像所有‘家庭斗争’最好只留给家庭成员，不要向公众透露太多。我认为这是他的委员会同事（和市场参与者）发现问题的第二部分，并且不符合先例。委员会成员显然愿意公开表达不同意见，或者至少比沃什主席更公开地表达他们的意见。这也是他逃避外部压力（总统、国会、市场）的责任的一种方式。”

摩根大通首席美国经济学家迈克尔·费罗利（Michael Feroli）：

“我猜家庭斗争是他试图变得平易近人，但这并没有背离[本]伯南克（公开）欢迎不同意见的传统。”

“这已经很模糊了，无论沃什想要表达什么意思，但从上下文来看，这似乎是他所表现出的决策和结构性改革的基础。今年夏天（在辛特拉），他表示，他的中央银行领导同事在质疑货币政策制定的整个过程时，都‘愿意回到首要原则’。我认为他重新引入货币总量，他可爱的“货币政策应该与货币有关”，都是从同一个一般性原则中删除的。布作为“首要原则”。可悲的是，货币政策科学和货币政策的其他主力（菲利普斯曲线）、经济预测甚至在沃什的第一个原则性方法中似乎毫无帮助。他并不羞于抨击通货膨胀年复一年的失利，并指责杰罗姆·鲍威尔和他的公司。低利率水平、资产负债表、未能尽快收紧货币政策、FAIT 等等，所有这些（对沃什来说）都背离了‘首要原则’。”

“‘第一原则’是‘质疑一切’的代号。沃什多次表示，他希望美联储进行“政权变革”，并质疑货币政策实施方式是否符合这一议程的基本假设。我怀疑沃什能否重写第一原则。仅表明假设有缺陷是不够的；它需要提供更好的替代品。即使有了他的特别工作组，沃什也可能无法实现新的首要原则——货币政策不会发生政权更迭，而是对其实施方式进行一些渐进的改进。”

“重新思考美联储实现价格稳定和充分就业双重使命目标的方式，不要有先入为主的观念或假设，也不要因为可能与当前方法不同而排除其他方法。首先考虑沟通、通胀和劳动力市场评估、资产负债表和运营框架以及数据源的最佳方法是什么。然后考虑如何过渡到这些新方法。”

“请注意，委员会往往不想做出重大改变——考虑一下声明仅改变一两个词的次数。这种对首要原则的重新思考使委员会能够考虑新的方法，而不是进行细微的调整。然后他们将不得不考虑如何最好地过渡到那里。”

“‘第一原则’路线感觉与他关于精英机构的博士不将资金保留在货币政策中的言论具有相同的精神。两者似乎都表明，该机构让学术驱动型的人分散了美联储对一些基本经济原则的关注。（鲍威​​尔也没有博士学位，但他对此似乎不那么防御性！）
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于财经市场信号，适合用于观察利率、汇率、股债商品、金融机构和宏观政策变化。
